Supercut · creative playbook

protein powder

What the longest-running paid social video ads in this market keep doing — counted, with the footage to prove it.

2026-07-12 · 60 ads studied across 44 brands

How we picked these ads

Advertisers kill losing ads fast, so an ad still delivering after a month has earned a look. We searched 216 ads in this market — including its heaviest spenders, so the pool reflects where real budgets go, not just what got views — and kept the ones running 30+ days and seen in the last 30. An AI watched each surviving ad and answered a fixed set of questions about it; code then counted the answers. 0 ads that turned out to sell something else were rejected — they are listed at the bottom. No single ad is called a winner here. The findings are patterns that repeat across 60 ads from 44 different brands.

What the studied ads sell

protein powder15 of 60
whey protein6 of 60
collagen powder4 of 60
amino acid supplements4 of 60
plant-based protein3 of 60
clear protein powder3 of 60
whey protein isolate3 of 60
protein bars2 of 60
20 one-off segments20 of 60

Top takeaways

48 of 60Prioritize showing the product in the very first three seconds of the video.
44 of 60Hire creators to film content instead of using polished brand shots.
59 of 60Ensure all ads are fully understandable even when the sound is muted.
18 of 20Use an energetic emotional tone to match successful market leaders.
  1. Use energetic creators to solve common protein problems ugc problem-solution · 20 ads
  2. Build trust through creator questions and social proof creator testimonial · 16 ads
  3. Let the visual of the product do the heavy lifting product demo · 9 ads
  4. Use fast pacing to show the product in daily life lifestyle montage · 7 ads
  5. Make bold claims with simple static images still image with text · 4 ads
  6. Use the founder to explain problems and products founder on camera · 4 ads
  7. What to test next
  8. What to double-check

Chapter 1 · ugc problem-solution

Use energetic creators to solve common protein problems

20 ads 18 brands 214.5 median days live 4 median cuts in first 10s 30s median length

Runs on Facebook (15) and TikTok (5).

This is the most common format in the market. It relies on real people talking about their fitness struggles and how a specific powder fits into their routine.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

social proof4 of 20
bold claim4 of 20
problem callout4 of 20
visual hook4 of 20
question2 of 20
curiosity tease1 of 20
offer or discount1 of 20

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds15 of 20
3 to 10 seconds5 of 20

How long they are

under 15s2 of 20
15-30s9 of 20
30-60s5 of 20
over 60s4 of 20

How they ask for the click

soft10 of 20
hard8 of 20
none2 of 20

The button under the ad

Shop now14 of 20
Learn more2 of 20
View now1 of 20
Check it out1 of 20
Order now1 of 20
Shop today1 of 20

How to shoot it

  • Show the product within the first three seconds. (15 of 20 ads)
  • Direct the creator to maintain an energetic tone. (18 of 20 ads)
  • Include a full demonstration of how to use the powder. (16 of 20 ads)
  • Keep the final video length around 30 seconds. (9 of 20 ads)
  • Use a medium pacing style for the edit. (14 of 20 ads)

Examples that lasted

Serena Neel
Opens with: “So a lot of people are confused why I bought a hundred orange juices.” · 518 days live · 10.0M plays · watch the ad
hauskris
Opens with: “A lot of people say that their protein shake recipe tastes like a milkshake” · 400 days live · 2.7M plays · watch the ad
Claire Koepnick
Opens with: “as someone who works out first thing in the morning” · 180 days live · 78k plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Only one ad in this group used humor, so stick to direct energetic or sincere approaches.

Chapter 2 · creator testimonial

Build trust through creator questions and social proof

16 ads 13 brands 184 median days live 1 median cuts in first 10s 28s median length

Runs on Facebook (10) and TikTok (6).

These ads focus on the creator's personal experience. They often start by asking a question or citing social proof to grab attention.

How to shoot it

  • Start the ad with a question to the audience. (5 of 16 ads)
  • Start the ad with a social proof hook. (5 of 16 ads)
  • Featured a full demo of the product being mixed or consumed. (11 of 16 ads)
  • Ensure the feel remains strictly that of a creator, not a brand. (15 of 16 ads)

Chapter 3 · product demo

Let the visual of the product do the heavy lifting

9 ads 6 brands 174 median days live 9 median cuts in first 10s 26s median length

Runs on Facebook (5) and TikTok (4).

This format uses fast pacing and visual hooks to show the product in action. It is effective for showing texture and mixability.

How to shoot it

  • Open with a strong visual hook to grab attention. (6 of 9 ads)
  • Include a full demonstration of the powder. (6 of 9 ads)
  • Maintain an energetic emotional tone throughout. (6 of 9 ads)
  • Show the product on screen within the first three seconds. (7 of 9 ads)

None of these ads require sound to be understood.

Chapter 4 · lifestyle montage

Use fast pacing to show the product in daily life

7 ads 7 brands 180 median days live 10 median cuts in first 10s 26s median length

Runs on TikTok (4) and Facebook (3).

Montages work by showing many quick clips of the product being used in different settings. They are very popular on TikTok.

How to shoot it

  • Use fast pacing with high cut density. (6 of 7 ads)
  • Keep the tone energetic through every clip. (7 of 7 ads)
  • Ensure the product is visible in the first three seconds. (6 of 7 ads)
  • Use a visual hook to start the sequence. (4 of 7 ads)

Chapter 5 · still image with text

Make bold claims with simple static images

4 ads 3 brands 183.5 median days live 0 median cuts in first 10s 7s median length

This format lives on Facebook: 4 of 4 ads.

Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

These are the simplest ads, using a single image and text overlays. They are exclusively found on Facebook in this pool.

How to shoot it

  • Feature a bold claim as the primary hook. (3 of 4 ads)
  • Ensure the brand feel is prominent. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Keep the length under 15 seconds. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Show the product clearly for the entire duration. (4 of 4 ads)

Opens with: “CREATIVE FLAVORS THAT NAIL IT EVERY TIME!” · 194 days live · 0 plays · watch the ad

Watch out

None of these ads include a product demonstration.

Chapter 6 · founder on camera

Use the founder to explain problems and products

4 ads 3 brands 204 median days live 4 median cuts in first 10s 23s median length

Runs on Facebook (3) and TikTok (1).

Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

Founders speaking directly to the camera can bridge the gap between creator and brand feel. These ads are often energetic and fast-paced.

  • Record the founder in an energetic tone. (3 of 4 ads)

What to test next

Energetic Founder Hook Test

Hypothesis
If we use the founder to lead with a bold claim in an energetic tone, we expect higher engagement than a sincere tone.
Why this test
Successful founder ads show a preference for energetic tones, with 3 out of 4 ads using this style.
Call it on
CTR
Shot notes
  • Record founder in bright natural light
  • Capture founder making a bold claim about protein quality
  • Keep the pacing fast with at least 4 cuts in 10 seconds
Risk
The audience may find the energy level insincere coming from a founder.
Draft script
Stop settled for chalky protein! Our new whey isolate is the smoothest on the market. Get yours now.

Speed-to-Product Visual Test

Hypothesis
If we show the product in the first 3 seconds, we expect higher retention than waiting until later.
Why this test
There is a massive trend of showing the product early, with 15 out of 20 UGC ads doing so.
Call it on
Hook Rate
Shot notes
  • Hold the protein tub next to face in opening shot
  • Use a bright, high-contrast label
  • Keep the creator energetic
Risk
Showing the product too early can feel like a traditional commercial.
Draft script
I finally found it. This is the protein powder that actually tastes like a milkshake. Check it out.

UGC Social Proof Hook Test

Hypothesis
If we open a UGC ad with social proof, we will outperform curiosity-based hooks.
Why this test
Social proof and bold claims are tied for the most popular hooks in the pool at 12 and 13 ads respectively, while curiosity teases only have 2.
Call it on
Conversion Rate
Shot notes
  • Start with 'Over 10,000 five-star reviews' text overlay
  • Have creator show the product tub immediately
  • Demonstrate the powder being mixed in a clear glass
Risk
Social proof hooks can feel repetitive if the audience has seen similar ads.
Draft script
There is a reason this powder has 10,000 five-star reviews. It actually mixes in 5 seconds. Shop now.

What to double-check

Everything above comes from an AI watching ads one at a time and code counting its answers. Here is where that process was noisy, and what it threw out.

Ads rejected as off-market (0)

The search also returned ads selling something else. They were dropped before any counting, so they do not touch the numbers above.

Nothing was rejected in this run.

How reliable was the watching?

We measured the editing pace of every ad twice: once by the AI's eye, once by scene-detection software. The AI was off by 2.5 cuts on average, and within 2 cuts of the software on 60% of ads. The cut counts printed in the chapters are the software's, not the AI's.

Where a spoken hook could be checked against the ad library's own transcript (23 ads), the AI's quote matched 23 times and disagreed 0 times. The other 37 ads had on-screen-text hooks, no hook, or no transcript — nothing to check against.

10 of 60 ads were described by the AI as making no ask, yet the ad unit itself carries a purchase button (ads b802053c2b99480d5be4, f66d9c73952b213626aca, f4cb12b210bc99cd00cd2, ff9bbc7d5947d67450b09, d622a38cdd5e6fea7a36, b4b34050fd5ce6040045, f1d571607ff8ffd8246db, f9f85074491c214afbea3, b13c9ab4748e89cc7abb, afc8513033f430404a49). Read those "no ask" counts with that in mind: the AI judges the video, not the button under it.

The AI reported firm format labels for every ad in the pool.

Assumptions this report makes

  • "Still delivering" means the ad was seen in the ad library within the last 30 days. The data offers no direct "currently live" flag.
  • Days-running and play counts come from the ad library and are taken at face value.
  • A long-running ad earned study, but nothing here proves any single ad was profitable. Only the repeated patterns are conclusions.
  • Spoken quotes were transcribed by the AI from the audio; where the ad library had its own transcript, we cross-checked (see above).

Study ranking: ads are ordered for the example lists by a score of longevity ×0.5 + engagement ×0.3 + formatConfidence ×0.2, each part normalized within this pool, with at most one ad per brand in any list. Engagement is measured as a rate — likes and shares per play, a share counting 5× a like — because plays on a paid ad are bought, so raw counts would mostly rank budgets. Rates from under 10,000 plays are ignored as noise.

Made with Supercut. Days-running and play counts come from the ad library data; "still delivering" means seen in the last 30 days, the closest signal the data offers to "currently live".
